    "R1.3": "The superlative claim 'strongest published result on this benchmark to date' is the central empirical contribution claim and is directly falsified by the number cited two paragraphs earlier (91.2% vs. 87.4%). Evidence-claim alignment is therefore load-bearing.",
    "R1.4": "The novelty argument rests on single-pass evidential abstention outperforming deep-ensemble baselines in quality-at-coverage; if the AUROC figure is wrong or the comparison set is incomplete, the claimed delta collapses. Central but contingent on resolving R1.3.",

rationale.R1.2: The section asserts RadAbstain achieves '87.4% AUROC … the strongest published result' while citing Liu and Zhang (2019) at 91.2% AUROC under the identical coverage condition. 87.4 < 91.2 is a direct logical contradiction internal to the text; this cross-section incoherence must be flagged regardless of the help modality.
rationale.R1.3: The superlative claim 'strongest published result on this benchmark to date' is the central empirical contribution claim and is directly falsified by the number cited two paragraphs earlier (91.2% vs. 87.4%). Evidence-claim alignment is therefore load-bearing.
rationale.R1.4: The novelty argument rests on single-pass evidential abstention outperforming deep-ensemble baselines in quality-at-coverage; if the AUROC figure is wrong or the comparison set is incomplete, the claimed delta collapses. Central but contingent on resolving R1.3.
rationale.R1.6: 'Help me with this section' most commonly signals prose-level assistance; the writing has some clarity issues (e.g., 'K-fold inference cost' is imprecise for ensembles, the transition from related work to contribution is abrupt). Central if the delegation is writing-focused.

parsed.clarification_question: Are you asking for prose and writing improvements (sentence clarity, flow, word choice) rather than a substantive scientific review (checking whether the claims, numbers, and novelty arguments are correct)?
